video_title: Oil In Focus As US-Iran Peace Talks Show Signs of Progress | The Asia Trade 6/22/2026
gist: Bloomberg's Asia Trade frames a fragile US-Iran interim deal (the 'MoU') that markets have largely priced as done, even as Trump threatens fresh strikes and the Strait of Hormuz reopening, tolls, and a Lebanon/Hezbollah ceasefire remain unresolved. Guests argue the bigger structural stories are a hawkish, Warsh-led Fed, a yen near 40-year lows driven by FX-hedging flows off Japan's equity boom, and a structural (not cyclical) Asian AI-memory rally led by Korea/Japan HBM suppliers.

insights.claim: The US-Iran interim deal (MoU) is essentially an attempt to return to the pre-war status quo and represents a strategic defeat for the United States, since Hormuz was already open before the war and the nuclear issue is merely deferred ~60 days.
insights.speaker: Kelly, senior fellow at the Stimson Center
insights.so_what: If the 'peace' merely restores the status quo with no nuclear resolution, the deal carries high re-escalation risk and oil should retain a geopolitical premium rather than fully normalize.
insights.quote: THIS IS VERY MUCH A STRATEGIC DEFEAT FOR THE UNITED STATES.

insights.claim: The interim Iran deal is very fragile because Trump repeatedly throws out remarks threatening fresh strikes if Iran does not stop Hezbollah, irritating Iranian negotiators and setting the wrong tone for talks that already saw the Iranians briefly walk out.
insights.speaker: Wendy, Bloomberg senior editor
insights.so_what: Headline risk from a single Trump post can reprice oil and risk assets intraday, so volatility around the 60-day window should be hedged.
insights.quote: I WOULD SAY THE DEAL IS VERY FRAGILE AT THE MOMENT

insights.claim: Geopolitical agreement and economic normalization in the Gulf are on completely different timelines because the Strait of Hormuz still has mines in the water and no one will move supertankers through until clearing operations finish, plus idled production capacity must be restarted.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: Even a signed deal leaves a multi-month gap before oil flow normalizes, supporting a sustained near-term floor under crude and shipping/insurance costs.
insights.quote: THE STRAIT STILL HAS MINES IN THE WATER. I FEEL LIKE NO ONE IS MOVING SUPERTANKERS THROUGH THERE UNTIL THE CLEARING OPERATIONS ARE DONE.

insights.claim: Despite Iranian rhetoric that the Strait is closed, physical oil shipping shows the Strait of Hormuz is more porous than claimed, with five tankers exiting over the weekend and Iran's main export terminal loading the most supertankers in six weeks.
insights.speaker: Nicholas, Bloomberg oil reporter
insights.so_what: Tape-reading on tanker traffic, not headlines, is the better real-time signal for whether the oil supply shock is actually easing.
insights.quote: DESPITE THE RHETORIC AND THE HORMUZ BEING A RHETORICAL DEVICE TO REFLECT ON HOW THE TWO PARTIES ARE, TRAFFIC IS STILL FLOWING.

insights.claim: Whether Iran reimposes tolls or other charges on the Strait of Hormuz after the 60-day window is unresolved and is a key variable markets have not priced.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Hormuz transit tolls would be a persistent tax on Gulf oil flows, structurally lifting freight and crude costs even under a 'peace' scenario.

insights.claim: The Lebanon/Hezbollah ceasefire is the crux on which the whole Iran deal rests, because Iran has stated the memorandum will not hold if the Hezbollah-Israel ceasefire breaks, yet Israel is not at the talks and vows to keep its campaign against Lebanon.
insights.speaker: Wendy / Michael Heath, Bloomberg editors
insights.so_what: A break in the Lebanon ceasefire is the single most likely trigger to collapse the deal and spike oil, making Lebanon headlines the key watch item.
insights.quote: IRAN HAS SAID EXCLUSIVELY THAT THE MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING WILL NOT HOLD IF THE CEASE-FIRE BETWEEN HEZBOLLAH AND ISRAEL IS BROKEN.

insights.claim: A potential break in US-Israeli relations is possible because US and Israeli interests have diverged, with Vance reminding Israel that two-thirds of the defensive systems protecting it during the war were American-made weapons as implicit leverage.
insights.speaker: Kelly, Stimson Center
insights.so_what: A visible US-Israel rift would raise Middle East tail risk and could pressure defense and oil trades depending on which way Israel chooses.
insights.quote: TWO THIRDS OF THE DEFENSIVE SYSTEMS THAT HAVE PROTECTED ISRAEL DURING THE WAR WITH IRAN HAVE ACTUALLY BEEN AMERICAN-MADE WEAPONS.

insights.claim: The nuclear issue was barely mentioned and placed far down in the agreement even though the war was ostensibly started over Iran's nuclear program, with no elimination of the program and outstanding questions on enriched uranium and monitoring.
insights.speaker: Kelly, Stimson Center
insights.so_what: An unresolved nuclear file means a recurring future flashpoint, keeping a structural risk premium in Middle East-exposed assets.

insights.claim: Markets have reacted cautiously rather than aggressively repricing the Iran deal, with crude up only ~3% and Nasdaq 100 futures little changed, signaling traders see the agreement as fragile but not fragile enough to warrant an aggressive repricing.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: Muted cross-asset reaction implies asymmetric downside if the deal breaks, since complacency leaves room for a sharp repricing.
insights.quote: WE SEE CRUDE UP AROUND 3%, AND THE NASDAQ 100 FUTURES ARE LITTLE CHANGED.

insights.claim: A Kevin Warsh-led Fed is leaning hawkish and could hike another 25 basis points by year-end, a sharp divergence from the March meeting when markets expected a 25bp cut.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: A pivot from expected cuts to hikes would support the US dollar and pressure rate-sensitive and EM assets; watch the Fed's preferred inflation gauge due Thursday.
insights.quote: KEVIN WARSH INDICATED THAT THE FED IS LEANING TO A HAWKISH SIDE AND THE FED COULD HIKE BY THE END OF THIS YEAR ANOTHER 25 BASIS POINTS

insights.claim: Bond markets are bear-flattening (yields up across the curve, more at the short end) reflecting a hawkish pivot from central bankers who remain worried about long-term inflation even if oil prices fall.
insights.speaker: Garfield Reynolds, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: Persistent bear-flattening signals positioning for higher-for-longer policy, favoring short-duration and hurting long-duration bond holders.
insights.quote: THE BEAR FLATTENING WE HAVE SEEN IN THE BOND MARKETS, MEANING YIELDS ARE GOING UP ACROSS THE CURVE. MORE AT THE SHORT END IS ABOUT THIS HAWKISH PIVOT

insights.claim: Increased government bond issuance is looming as governments move to replenish defense stocks or adapt to lessons from the Gulf War about the changing nature of combat, adding supply pressure to the long end.
insights.speaker: Garfield Reynolds, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: Higher defense-driven issuance steepens long-end supply risk, a structural headwind for global long bonds and a tailwind for defense names.

insights.claim: Westpac expects oil prices will not return to pre-conflict levels, keeping core inflation elevated and food a key pressure point even as headline inflation drops on lower energy.
insights.speaker: Sian Fenner, head of business economics at Westpac
insights.so_what: A higher oil floor plus sticky core/food inflation supports the hawkish central bank bias and argues against pricing aggressive rate cuts.
insights.quote: WE ARE NOT EXPECTING PRICES TO RETURN TO PRE-CONFLICT LEVELS.

insights.claim: Westpac forecasts Australian inflation rising toward roughly 3.8% in the second half and expects two more RBA rate hikes, with the first move expected around August.
insights.speaker: Sian Fenner, Westpac
insights.so_what: A clearly hawkish RBA path supports AUD rates and challenges any dovish positioning in Australian fixed income.
insights.quote: WE ARE LOOKING FOR TWO MORE RATE HIKES.

insights.claim: US tariffs on China remain at peak levels with no reversal, and sector-specific measures including pharmaceuticals tariffs and China's beef safeguards (impacting Brazilian beef) are proving durable despite legal setbacks.
insights.speaker: Sian Fenner, Westpac
insights.so_what: Sticky tariffs sustain supply-side cost pressure and reinforce the inflation/hawkish theme; pharma and protected commodities face lasting trade friction.

insights.claim: The yen's weakness toward 40-year lows (~161.44, approaching the 1986 low of 161.95) is driven less by the BOJ being behind the curve and more by FX hedging tied to foreign investors' Japanese equity holdings, which forces yen selling as Japanese stocks rise.
insights.speaker: Shusuke, Bank of America Global Research
insights.so_what: If the driver is equity-hedge flow, the yen turns only when Japan's equity outperformance stalls, so watch relative Nikkei vs S&P performance over FX intervention rhetoric.
insights.quote: OVER THE PAST YEAR, IT HAS REALLY BEEN FX HEDGING DO TO EQUITY HOLDINGS BY FOREIGN INVESTORS BECAUSE OF SIGNIFICANT OUTPERFORMANCE OF JAPANESE EQUITIES.

insights.claim: Since the rally started last April the Nikkei has roughly doubled while the S&P 500 rose just 30%, and with foreign investors holding ~$2 trillion in Japanese equities the hedge-rebalancing may have generated hundreds of billions of dollars of excess yen selling.
insights.speaker: Shusuke, Bank of America Global Research
insights.so_what: Quantifies the mechanical yen-selling flow; a Japanese equity correction could unwind it and snap the yen sharply higher.
insights.quote: THE NIKKEI HAS BASICALLY DOUBLED. S&P 500 ROSE JUST BY 30%.

insights.claim: Bank of America turned constructive on the yen last month, shifting its year-end target from 157 to 152 and expecting the next BOJ hike in October, with falling oil prices removing one of three negative yen catalysts (BOJ, fiscal, oil).
insights.speaker: Shusuke, Bank of America Global Research
insights.so_what: A named bull target (152) and the three-catalyst framework give a concrete way to track when to fade yen shorts.
insights.quote: WE SHIFTED THE FOCUS FROM 157 TO 152 FOR THE YEN.

insights.claim: Speculative yen short positioning at CME is stretched near all-time highs, suggesting the market is close to an inflection point where authorities may step up intervention.
insights.speaker: Shusuke, Bank of America Global Research
insights.so_what: Crowded shorts plus intervention risk create asymmetric squeeze potential for the yen in the near term.
insights.quote: THE YEN SHORT IS QUITE STRETCHED.

insights.claim: PM Takaichi can reshape the BOJ board toward reflationists as terms expire, replacing a departing member this month with a reflationist and potentially adding two more next year as the two biggest hawks finish their terms, making aggressive rate hikes questionable.
insights.speaker: Brian Fowler, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: A more dovish/reflationist board caps the BOJ tightening path, a structural negative for the yen even if a near-term hike comes in October.
insights.quote: TAKAICHI HAS THE CHANCE TO RESHAPE THE BOARD AS THE TERMS REACH THEIR LIMITS.

insights.claim: A Bloomberg survey shows economists see the BOJ terminal rate this cycle at about 1.75%, implying roughly three more hikes are needed.
insights.speaker: Brian Fowler, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: Anchors expectations for the BOJ path; deviations from a ~1.75% terminal rate would move JGBs and the yen.
insights.quote: THE TERMINAL RATE FOR THIS CYCLE WILL BE ABOUT 1.75.

insights.claim: Asia holds the upper hand in the AI trade via the memory stack, with the three HBM suppliers controlling roughly 90% of global supply and two of the three based in Asia, giving them locked-in pricing power from industry structure rather than cleverness.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: Structural HBM oligopoly with 90% share supports durable pricing power, favoring Korean/Japanese memory names over cyclical interpretations.
insights.quote: THE GLOBAL HBM MARKET IS CLOSED. THEY CONTROL 90% OF GLOBAL SUPPLY. TWO OF THE THREE NAMES COME FROM ASIA.

insights.claim: Samsung and SK Hynix shares are pricing in profits as structural rather than cyclical, and Micron's earnings (due Wednesday) are the key test of whether memory demand visibility is truly multi-year.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stephens, guest
insights.so_what: Micron's guidance is the swing catalyst for the entire Asian memory complex; a cyclical message would force a derating of structurally-priced names.
insights.quote: SAMSUNG AND HYNIX HAVE BEEN PRICING IN PROFITS THAT ARE NOT CYCLICAL, THAT THEY ARE STRUCTURAL.

insights.claim: Tech is heavily overbought versus the broader market with the momentum trade very extended, and assets are flowing into ultrashort ETFs while leverage stays light, signaling positioning caution into Micron earnings.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stephens, guest
insights.so_what: Extended momentum plus rising short-side hedging warns of a sharp pullback risk if Micron disappoints.
insights.quote: THE TECH SPREAD VERSUS THE BROADER MARKET IS VERY EXTENDED. THE MOMENTUM TRADE IS VERY EXTENDED.

insights.claim: South Korean chip exports surged ~188% year-over-year in the first 20 days of the month, with total exports up 60.4%, underscoring AI/memory demand as the key driver of Northeast Asian markets.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Hard data confirming the AI demand pull-through into Korean trade balances supports the structural memory bull case.
insights.quote: CHIP EXPORTS ARE RISING -- I SHOULD SAY SURGING 180 8%.

insights.claim: There are concerns about the pricing power of frontier US AI versus newer Chinese AI, a competitive risk overhanging the US AI rally.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stephens, guest
insights.so_what: Erosion of US AI pricing power to Chinese rivals is a margin risk for frontier model and chip leaders to monitor.
insights.quote: YOU HAVE A LOT OF CONCERNS ABOUT THE PRICING POWER OF FRONTIER U.S. AI VERSUS THE NEW CHINESE AI.

insights.claim: LG Electronics executives are visiting Nvidia headquarters to discuss cooperation in physical AI and robotics, weeks after Nvidia's CEO visited the LG Group chairman.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Signals physical AI/robotics partnerships broadening the AI trade beyond pure memory into Korean industrials like LG.

insights.claim: SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic together carry a valuation of around $3.6 trillion, with SpaceX having completed what is described as the largest IPO in history and OpenAI and Anthropic having filed confidential listings.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: A wave of trillion-scale AI listings concentrates index exposure to unproven public-market earnings, raising systemic AI-concentration risk.
insights.quote: COMBINED, THE THREE COUNTRIES HAVE A VALUATION OF AROUND $3.6 TRILLION.

insights.claim: SpaceX could be added to the Nasdaq 100 within ~15 trading days, mechanically forcing index funds to buy a company with no full public earnings cycle, which could lead to distorted price discovery and a sharp reaction.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: Mechanical index inclusion of an unseasoned mega-cap is a structural risk for passive holders; the analyst is explicitly cautious on SpaceX.
insights.quote: THAT WOULD FORCE FUNDS TO BUY IN MECHANICALLY WITHOUT ADDING FUNDAMENTAL DUE DILIGENCE TO A COMPANY THAT HASN'T GENERATED A FULL EARNINGS CYCLE

insights.claim: An unprecedented amount of liquidity is being pulled out of public markets by these private AI listings, a second-order dynamic beyond the headline AI-boom valuation narrative.
insights.speaker: Dilin Wu, guest analyst
insights.so_what: Liquidity drain into a few mega-IPOs could pressure breadth elsewhere in public equities.

insights.claim: Emerging market companies are beating analyst estimates for the first time in four years, led by big-cap Asian tech with help from Latin American commodity producers, while EM trades at roughly a quarter of US semiconductor valuations.
insights.speaker: Anthony, Bloomberg markets reporter
insights.so_what: First earnings beats in four years plus a deep valuation discount give EM a fundamental, not just technical, catch-up case.
insights.quote: EMERGING MARKETS ARE BEATING ANALYST ESTIMATES NOW FOR THE FIRST TIME IN FOUR YEARS

insights.claim: Asian tech's rally is shifting from a cyclical version of the US tech trade to a structural multi-year growth story, with semiconductor-chain stocks across Taiwan, Korea and Hong Kong up more than 100% and the move broadening into construction and materials names.
insights.speaker: Anthony, Bloomberg markets reporter
insights.so_what: A cyclical-to-structural reframing justifies higher multiples for Asian tech and signals broadening beyond chips into broader macro plays.
insights.quote: THE ARGUMENT HAS CHANGED TO THE FACT THAT THIS TECH TRADE HAS BECOME STRUCTURAL

insights.claim: The EM story is no longer singular: Korea and Taiwan produce memory the US absolutely needs (a structural edge), while Indonesia, the Philippines and India remain challenged by policy mistakes and, for India, undiversified energy supply.
insights.speaker: Anthony, Bloomberg markets reporter
insights.so_what: Favors hard-tech EMs (Korea/Taiwan) over policy- and energy-vulnerable EMs; selectivity matters more than a blanket EM call.
insights.quote: TAIWAN AND KOREA SIT ASTRIDE PRETTY MUCH UNBEATABLE NOTES IN MEMORY

insights.claim: China's selloff is weighing on emerging markets, with MSCI China on the cusp of a bear market amid weak growth, underperforming internet and consumer firms, and consumption that never recovered from the property crisis.
insights.speaker: Anthony / host, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: China as the biggest overhang on EM means the asset class's sustainable bull run depends on Chinese consumption stabilizing.
insights.quote: MSCI CHEN ON THE CUSP OF A BEAR MARKET AMID CONCERNS ABOUT CHINA'S GROWTH OUTLOOK

insights.claim: Emerging markets like Indonesia, India and the Philippines face the question of how many rate hikes their economies can absorb as a stronger dollar and rising oil force defensive tightening.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stephens, guest
insights.so_what: Rate-hike capacity is the binding constraint for vulnerable EMs; their underperformance can persist until oil and the dollar ease.
insights.quote: HOW MANY RATE HIKES CANDLES MARKETS TAKE? THAT IS A BIG QUESTION.

themes.theme: Macro wildcards: El Nino, UK, Colombia

insights.claim: Scientists put a 63% probability on a super El Nino heading into 2027, expected to hurt crop yields and reignite inflation, benefiting agriculture input and fertilizer makers and select insurers while hurting palm oil, wheat and grain producers.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg senior Asia equities reporter
insights.so_what: A super El Nino adds a fresh food-inflation upside risk reinforcing the hawkish theme; trade it long ag-inputs/fertilizer and watch crop producers and ag lenders.
insights.quote: SCIENTISTS ARE EXPECTING 63% OF A SUPER EVEN

insights.claim: The UK pound faces extended losses as PM Starmer is expected to announce a path to step aside, paving the way for Andy Burnham to become the seventh UK prime minister in a decade, with bond markets mattering more than currency markets for the leadership change.
insights.speaker: Garfield Reynolds / Rosalind Mathieson, Bloomberg
insights.so_what: The gilt market reaction to Burnham's fiscal stance and chancellor pick is the key risk; a soft-left, higher-spend signal could spook UK bonds.
insights.quote: WHAT MATTERS MOST IS NOT SO MUCH WHAT THE CURRENCY MARKETS THINK, BUT WHAT THE BOND MARKETS THINK.

insights.claim: A Trump-endorsed conservative narrowly leads Colombia's presidential vote (~58% of initial count) and has promised to reopen oil drilling (drill, baby, drill), which could juice the economy of South America's third-largest, though the close result may dampen investor optimism.
insights.speaker: Clayton Harrison, Bloomberg Latin America executive editor
insights.so_what: A pro-drilling Colombian win could lift oil supply and Colombian assets while easing fiscal concerns, but a contested narrow result is a risk to that optimism.
insights.quote: COLUMBIA HAS STOPPED A NEW DRILLING FOR OIL. AND THEY HAVE PROMISED DRILL, BABY, DRILL.

insights.claim: China's exports of certain tungsten types and some rare earths to Japan remained at zero in May amid a political dispute, extending export controls imposed in January on dual-use items shipped to Tokyo.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Continued zero critical-mineral flows to Japan is a supply risk for Japanese industrial and tech supply chains and a geopolitical lever to watch.
insights.quote: EXPORTS OF CERTAIN TYPES OF TUNGSTEN AND SOME RARE EARTHS REMAINS AT ZERO

insights.claim: WiseTech shares fell about 11% as executive chairman Richard White was embroiled in a probe over claims he exploited a woman's immigration status and provided false information on a visa application, amid prolonged governance concerns at the company.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Governance and founder-conduct risk at one of Australia's top tech names is a single-stock overhang for AUD tech holders.
